islands.name: Intangible Isle
islands.description: Explore the invisible assets: intellectual property, brand value, goodwill, and patents.

learning_objectives: Understand intellectual property: patents, trademarks, copyrights
Learn about brand value and goodwill as intangible assets
Explore how intangible assets are valued on a balance sheet
provenance.originality_notes: Theme: mysterious isle of ideas and invisible treasures (not a law office). Verb: categorize and match intangible concepts through NPC scenarios (not lecture-based). Structure: discovery quests that reveal hidden value in everyday brands (not exam prep). Combines sorting puzzles with dialogue exploration.

areas.name: Patent Office
areas.description: Rows of filing cabinets and inventors pitching ideas. Learn how IP creates value.

areas.name: Brand Boulevard
areas.description: Billboards and logos everywhere. Discover why a name can be worth billions.

areas.name: Goodwill Garden
areas.description: A peaceful park where deals are made. Understand goodwill in acquisitions.

items.name: Patent Certificate
items.description: A patent protects your invention for 20 years. Nobody else can copy it.

items.name: Brand Valuation Report
items.description: Analysis showing how brand recognition translates into revenue.

items.name: Goodwill Analysis
items.description: Shows the premium paid in an acquisition above the tangible asset value.

items.name: Intangible Isle Medallion
items.description: You understand the hidden value of intangible assets!

quests.title: IP Creator
quests.description: File a mock patent, learn about trademarks and copyrights, and see how IP drives company value.
quests.hint: Talk to Inventor Iris at the Patent Office and consult Lawyer Leon about protection strategies.

quests.title: Brand Power & Goodwill
quests.description: Discover why companies pay billions for a brand name and what 'goodwill' means on a balance sheet.
quests.hint: Visit Branding Ben on Brand Boulevard, then find Merger Maya in Goodwill Garden.

nodes.speaker: Inventor Iris
nodes.text: I just invented a new gadget! But without a patent, anyone could copy it. Intellectual property — patents, trademarks, copyrights — protects creators. Want to learn how?

nodes.speaker: Inventor Iris
nodes.text: A patent protects inventions (20 years). A trademark protects brand names and logos (forever, if renewed). A copyright protects creative works like music and code (your lifetime + 70 years). Talk to Lawyer Leon for the legal details!

nodes.speaker: Inventor Iris
nodes.text: Patents = inventions. Trademarks = brand identity. Copyrights = creative works. Each has different rules and time limits. The important thing: IP can be your most valuable asset, even more than physical property!

choices.text: I want to protect my inventions!

nodes.speaker: Lawyer Leon
nodes.text: IP law is complex but crucial. Without protection, your ideas are free for anyone to steal. Ready to file a mock patent and see how the system works?

nodes.speaker: Lawyer Leon
nodes.text: A real patent costs $5,000-$15,000+ in fees and can take 2-3 years to approve. But if your invention makes millions, that's a tiny investment. The real cost is NOT patenting and watching someone else profit from your idea.

nodes.speaker: Branding Ben
nodes.text: Why is a plain white t-shirt $5, but the same shirt with a swoosh logo $45? Brand value! A strong brand lets you charge premium prices because customers trust the name.

choices.text: How much is a brand worth?

nodes.speaker: Branding Ben
nodes.text: Apple's brand alone is worth over $500 billion! Here's a brand valuation report showing how companies measure this invisible asset. Now visit Merger Maya to learn about goodwill.

nodes.speaker: Branding Ben
nodes.text: Absolutely. One scandal, one data breach, one terrible product can destroy decades of trust overnight. Brand value is powerful but fragile.

choices.text: Show me how brands are valued.

nodes.speaker: Merger Maya
nodes.text: When one company buys another, they often pay MORE than the sum of its assets. That 'extra' is called goodwill — it represents brand, customer relationships, patents, and team talent.

nodes.speaker: Merger Maya
nodes.text: Say a company has $10M in assets but is bought for $15M. That extra $5M is goodwill — the buyer believes the brand, customer base, and team are worth it. Here's an analysis report!

nodes.speaker: Merger Maya
nodes.text: Yes! If the acquired company underperforms, the buyer has to 'write down' goodwill — admitting they overpaid. It's a huge hit to the balance sheet. That's why due diligence matters.

minigames.name: IP Strategy Scenario
minigames.description: Navigate branching decisions about protecting your invention: patent, trademark, or open-source?
